exemplar.title:
How does agricultural runoff from farming affect invertebrate
biodiversity in ponds at Margam Discovery Center, South Wales?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does agricultural runoff from farming affect invertebrate
biodiversity in ponds at Margam Discovery Center, South Wales?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: The report is well structured with a clear table of contents, numbered headings and consistent formatting throughout.
Organization aids navigation and supports reader engagement from context through to bibliography.
weaknesses: Prose is often wordy and repetitive, reducing concision.
Terminology is appropriate but not always used concisely.
Logical flow is interrupted by blank table cells, inconsistent step numbering and some lapses in figure referencing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: COM strands are 3 (organization), 2 (terminology/concision) and 2 (logic/coherence).  While structure and labeling are excellent, the writing lacks concision and occasionally hinders coherence, placing the overall communication at band 2.

strengths: The research question is focused, geographically specific and clearly states both cause and effect.
A thorough discussion of eutrophication, nitrate loading, hypoxia and global fertilizer trends situates the study in a broad context.
Connections between agricultural runoff, algal blooms, oxygen depletion and biodiversity loss are explained comprehensively.
weaknesses: Minor wording redundancies remain in the research question formulation.
The narrative lacks explicit reference to key environmental policies (e.g., EU Nitrates Directive).
One sentence in the local connections section is incomplete, interrupting flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three CXT strands clearly meet the 5â6 band: the research question is coherent and wellâfocused, the environmental issue is discussed in depth with quantitative detail, and the links between issue and question are thoroughly explained.  Although minor improvements (policy linkage, sentence completion) remain, the submission fulfils the full descriptor for band 6.

strengths: The method is repeatable and appropriate, with clear stepâbyâstep procedures and equipment details.
Sampling strategy is justified with 20 random sweepânet samples and rationale contrasted with alternatives.
Risk and ethical considerations are comprehensively addressed with likelihood and severity tables and mitigation measures.
weaknesses: Only one nitrate and pH measurement per pond limits statistical powerâreplicates are needed.
Depth control instructions (â10 m downwardâ) are ambiguous and need precise specification.
Control of confounding variables (e.g., depth variation) lacks a detailed methodology.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: PLA strands are assessed at 3â4 (method design) and 5â6 (sampling strategy, risk/ethics).  The student shows strong justification and comprehensive risk planning, but the critical gap in abiotic sampling and minor procedural ambiguities prevent full band 6.  Overall, PLA aligns best with band 5.

strengths: Identifies relevant strengths and weaknesses of the method (sample size, randomization, species ID bias).
Offers meaningful suggestions (seasonal replication, multiâsite comparison, advanced sampling equipment).
Addresses limitations and proposes concrete extensions that directly target weaknesses.
weaknesses: Evaluation of the conclusion in environmental context is descriptive rather than systematic.
Discussion of confounding variables and spatial scale is mentioned but not fully integrated.
Improvements stop short of major redesigns (e.g., longitudinal studies, alternative biodiversity indices).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: DEV strands are all in the midâband (3â4): the student evaluates methods and proposes plausible improvements but lacks a fully systematic appraisal of the conclusion and deeper contextual critique.  The balance of descriptive evaluation and meaningful extensions places the work in band 4 overall.

strengths: Appropriate tables, summary charts and a cumulativeâmean graph present key data clearly.
Statistical analysis (means, SDI, tâtests) is calculated and interpreted correctly.
The conclusion is well supported by SDI values, abiotic measurements and statistical outcomes, linking trends to ecological mechanisms.
weaknesses: Several cells in abiotic tables (nitrate, pH) are blank, impeding full data presentation.
Some key visuals (box plots, error bars) are missing to illustrate variability.
The significance threshold (e.g., p<0.05) is not stated when reporting tâtest results.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: RAC strands fall into 3â4 (presentation, analysis) and 5â6 (conclusion).  Despite omissions in table completeness and missing graphical elements, the student constructs relevant visuals, performs correct analyses and draws a fully supported conclusion.  The strength of interpretation justifies a bandâ5 award.

strengths: Proposes a bufferâzone solution clearly linked to findings on nitrate runoff.
Justifies the application with mechanism, practicality and anticipated ecological benefits.
Demonstrates understanding of how the buffer zone mitigates key environmental issues.
weaknesses: Evaluation of strengths and weaknesses omits social, economic and policy tradeâoffs.
Longâterm maintenance, monitoring costs and stakeholder engagement are not discussed.
Limited exploration of unintended consequences or alternative management options.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: APP meets band 3 for a wellâjustified application proposal, and partial evaluation of solution strengths/weaknesses.  Although social and economic dimensions are underexplored, the core justification based on empirical findings satisfies the highest band descriptor for application.

strandSelections.reasoning: Specialist terms (eutrophication, SDI, saprobiont) are consistently used, yet the prose is often wordy and repetitive, and some tables contain redundant headings.  Thus the work fulfils either terminology or concision, but not both simultaneouslyâearning 2 marks.

The evaluation restates key findings and affirms their environmental relevance but offers limited critical scrutiny (e.g., scale, confounding variables).  Discussion of nitrate sampling limitation appears later but is not integrated into a systematic weighing of evidence.  This represents a descriptive, partly evaluative approach â mid band.

The conclusion clearly cites SDI values, nitrate levels, dissolved oxygen means and t-test outcomes to justify rejecting/accepting hypotheses.  Links between data trends and ecological mechanisms are explicit, and temperature non-significance is acknowledged.  Support is strong, with only minor oversights, suiting 5 marks.

The student calculates means, SDI, cumulative means and two t-tests, interpreting significance correctly.  However, nitrate and pH data are not analysed statistically, and no error bars or variability measures accompany biodiversity indices.  The analysis is correct yet partial, warranting the upper-middle band.

For each weakness the student suggests an improvement (e.g., species ID apps, Van Dorn bottle, seasonal replication, phosphate testing).  Suggestions are meaningful and address causes, yet they stop short of significant redesigns or novel extensions; hence the mid-band score.

Step-by-step procedures, equipment details and 20 random sweep-net samples indicate repeatability, and the method would generate biodiversity and abiotic data.  However, only one nitrate and pH reading per pond was planned, leaving a critical variable insufficiently sampled, and the timing description ("10 m downward") is ambiguous.  These gaps prevent full sufficiency, so the strand fits solidly in the 3-4 band.

Connections are explained rather than merely listed: the student traces how regional fertilizer application elevates nitrate levels, triggers algal blooms, lowers dissolved oxygen and ultimately reduces invertebrate diversity; local employment data and SAC phosphorus failures are used as supporting evidence.  The explanation is largely complete, with minimal omissions, aligning with the comprehensive descriptor.

The research question is explicitly stated, geographically delimited (Margam Discovery Centre, South-Wales) and specifies both the cause (agricultural runoff) and the response variable (invertebrate biodiversity).  It is neither overly broad nor ambiguous, allowing a clear cause-and-effect investigation.  Minor wording redundancies remain, but these do not undermine focus or coherence; therefore the work best fits the 5-6 band.

The student identifies key strengths (filtration, ease of establishment) and weaknesses (cost, limited spatial coverage) of buffer zones, but economic, social and temporal dimensions are only briefly touched upon.  Several relevant trade-offs remain unexplored, so the evaluation fits the âsomeâ rather than âallâ descriptor.

A clear table of contents, numbered headings, labelled tables/figures and logical sequencing from context to bibliography demonstrate sustained organisation.  Navigation is easy throughout the report, meeting the top band descriptor.

A strengths-weaknesses table highlights sample size, randomisation, species misidentification, depth bias and debris issues.  While several points are relevant, not all major design limitations (single nitrate reading, lack of replicate sites) are explored, and depth of discussion is moderate rather than exhaustiveâplacing it in the 3-4 band.

The student gives a well-developed discussion of eutrophication, nitrate loading, hypoxia, and global trends in fertilizer use, integrating references and quantitative projections (e.g. food demand doubling by 2050).  The narrative clearly situates the local study within a wider environmental concern.  A slightly stronger link to specific conventions or policies could raise it to a 6, but the depth presented merits the upper band.

Random sampling, 20 equidistant points and justification for sweep-netting versus kick-sampling are all articulated, directly linking strategy to safety, representativeness and the research aim.  The rationale is explicit and persuasive, satisfying the requirement for justification (5-6 band), though a stratified element would be needed for full marks.

A structured table evaluates likelihood, severity and post-mitigation risk for six hazards, and a separate table addresses three ethical concerns with concrete solutions.  The coverage is thorough and specific to freshwater fieldwork, matching the âcomprehensiveâ description.

Extensive raw tables, summary tables, cumulativeâmean graph and photo documentation are provided.  Nonetheless, several tables contain empty cells (nitrate, pH) and some key visuals (SDI comparison, box plots) are absent.  Thus presentation is appropriate but incomplete, aligning with the 3-4 band.

The buffer-zone proposal is explicitly linked to findings on nitrate runoff, and justification includes mechanism, practicality and ecological benefit.  This satisfies the requirement for a justified solution (band 3).

Arguments progress logically, but occasional data omissions (blank cells), small lapses in figure referencing and some circular phrasing interrupt flow.  The report is mainly coherent yet difficult to follow in parts, corresponding to band 2.

annotations.comment: Only one nitrate and pH measurement per pond is planned, which limits statistical power; include replicate measurements across sampling points.
The risk assessment table is thorough, evaluating likelihood and severity for multiple hazards specific to freshwater fieldwork.
The term âuncertaintyâ in the risk calculation header is unclear; revise to âlikelihoodâ or âprobabilityâ to align with risk terminology.
Many cells in the abiotic factors table are blank for nitrate and pH, impeding comprehensive analysis; ensure all measurements are recorded.
Cumulative mean graphs lack axis labels and units; include clear axis descriptions to enhance interpretability.
The SDI equation is inverted; typically $$D=1-\frac{\sum n(n-1)}{N(N-1)}$$ or its reciprocal is used.  Verify and correct the formula.
The T-test table is dense and difficult to navigate; consider splitting into separate tables for each factor to improve clarity.
Overcrowded headers and columns obscure key results in the T-test; streamline header labels and reduce empty cells.
The research question is clearly articulated, geographically specific and focuses on cause and effect, demonstrating strong question formulation.
Consider tightening the phrasing of the research question to avoid redundancy (e.g., remove repeated terms around âaffectâ), improving concision.
The discussion of eutrophication, nitrate loading and hypoxia with quantitative projections provides excellent global context.
Link the narrative to specific environmental policies or conventions (e.g., EU Nitrates Directive) to deepen the contextual relevance.
The mechanism connecting algal decomposition by saprobionts to oxygen depletion and biodiversity loss is explained clearly.
Quantitatively relate the global food-demand projection to local nitrate loading rates at Margam to strengthen the link between global and local scales.
The site map lacks a scale bar and north arrow; adding these will improve spatial orientation and map credibility.
The paragraph in the local connections section ends abruptly (âAgriculturalâ); complete the sentence to maintain clarity.
The confounding variables table highlights relevant factors, but the control of depth lacks precise methodology; specify how depth variation will be measured.
Instruction âPlace the sweep net touching the bottom â¦ 10 meters downwardâ is ambiguous; specify exact depth and horizontal distance.
Step numbering is inconsistent (refers to stepping â1â13â but lists 15 steps); renumber the procedure to avoid confusion.
The conclusion synthesizes SDI, abiotic measurements and t-test findings effectively, supporting the hypothesis.
State the significance threshold (e.g., p<0.05) when reporting t-test results to contextualize statistical conclusions.
The evaluation section lacks systematic discussion of spatial scale and other confounding variables; integrate these to deepen insight.
Suggest significant methodological extensions such as seasonal replication and multi-site comparison to address temporal and spatial limitations.
Evaluation of the buffer-zone application notes cost and coverage but omits social and policy trade-offs critical for implementation.
Discuss long-term maintenance, monitoring costs and stakeholder engagement when proposing buffer zones to strengthen application evaluation.
The report is well structured with a clear table of contents and consistent headings, aiding navigation throughout.
Prose is often wordy and repetitive; streamline sentences and remove redundancies to improve readability and concision.
Occasional lapses in figure referencing and circular phrasing interrupt logical flow; cross-check all in-text references for coherence.
